section.title: Introduction
section.para: The package opennlp.tools.document provides a container that carries a piece of text together with every annotation produced from it. Where the individual tools in this manual each read one kind of input and return one kind of output, the document model gives those outputs a common home: a single immutable object that holds the original text and any number of typed annotation layers over it. Sentence boundaries, tokens, part-of-speech tags, names, and any layer an application defines all live side by side, each addressable by a typed key.

Two properties make the container useful as the backbone of a processing pipeline. First, every annotation is anchored to the original text by character offsets, and those offsets never change: a token span read back after ten further stages still indexes the exact substring it covered when it was produced. Second, the container is immutable. Adding a layer returns a new document rather than mutating the old one, so a document can be shared across threads without copying and a pipeline is a plain sequence of pure functions.

The chapters that follow describe the components that produce these layers: sentence detection, tokenization, part-of-speech tagging, name finding, and the rest. This chapter describes the container they share and the small interface an annotator implements to take part in a pipeline.

section.title: Documents and annotations
section.para: An annotation is a span of the text paired with a value. The span uses the same opennlp.tools.util.Span type used elsewhere in the library, and its start and end are offsets into document.text(). The value type is whatever the layer carries: a string for a token or a tag, a richer record for a structured layer. public record Annotation<T>(Span span, T value) { } Because the span always refers to the original text, an annotation produced by a later stage can be lined up with one from an earlier stage without any offset bookkeeping. A part-of-speech tag and the token it describes share the same span.

section.title: Layers and typed keys
section.para: A layer is identified by a LayerKey, which pairs a string identifier with the value type carried on that layer. The type parameter is what makes document.get(Layers.TOKENS) return a List<Annotation<String>> without a cast: the key carries the type, and the compiler checks that reads and writes agree. LayerKey<String> tokens = LayerKey.of("tokens", String.class); Two keys are equal when both their identifier and their type are equal, so a key can be reconstructed anywhere from the same identifier and class and will address the same layer.

The Layers class holds the keys the built-in annotators use, so applications and annotators refer to the same layers by the same constants:
Key Value
Layers.SENTENCES one annotation per sentence
Layers.TOKENS one annotation per token, value is the token text
Layers.POS_TAGS one annotation per token, value is the tag
Layers.ENTITIES one annotation per detected name, value is the type
An application defines its own layer by declaring a key of its own type. Nothing about the container is specific to the built-in layers; a custom layer is a first-class citizen addressed exactly like the built-in ones.

section.title: Annotators
section.para:
The library ships annotators that adapt the existing tools to this interface. Each wraps a trained model or component and declares its layers:
Annotator Requires Provides
SentenceDetectorAnnotator nothing SENTENCES
TokenizerAnnotator nothing; uses SENTENCES when present TOKENS
POSTaggerAnnotator TOKENS POS_TAGS
NameFinderAnnotator TOKENS ENTITIES
The tokenizer adapter shows the offset discipline the container relies on. When the sentence layer is present it tokenizes each sentence separately, then shifts each token span back into original-text coordinates, so a token span read from the finished document indexes the original text directly and not the sentence it was found in.

section.title: Running a pipeline
section.para:
A DocumentAnalyzer holds an ordered list of annotators and runs them over a piece of text. It is assembled with a builder: DocumentAnalyzer analyzer = DocumentAnalyzer.builder() .add(new SentenceDetectorAnnotator(sentenceDetector)) .add(new TokenizerAnnotator(tokenizer)) .add(new POSTaggerAnnotator(posTagger)) .build();
Document document = analyzer.analyze("Rockwell International Corp. said it signed an agreement."); The analyzer creates a document from the text and hands it to each annotator in turn, passing the document returned by one annotator to the next. Because each annotate call returns a new document, the analyzer simply threads the document through the sequence.

The builder checks the pipeline when it is built. Each annotator's requires set must be satisfied by the layers that earlier annotators provides, or the build fails rather than leaving a pipeline that would break at run time. Adding POSTaggerAnnotator before anything provides TOKENS, for example, is rejected at build time.

section.title: Writing a custom annotator
